humanities_role_explanation: Uses historical Czech archival texts as its dataset content, while being explicitly motivated by assisting historians and digital humanities researchers in tracing social and political themes across archives.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Text-only evaluations operating on transcribed Czech historical document segments obtained via PERO-OCR.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Topic presence detection (evaluating at the document level if a given topic is discussed) and span localization (identifying the exact word-level boundaries and disjoint or overlapping spans representing the topic).

general_purpose_benchmark.note: It is a specialized benchmark focusing specifically on Czech historical documents and topic localization.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates several generative LLMs including GPT-5 (gpt-5-2, gpt-5-mini, gpt-5-nano), Llama 3, Llama 3.2, Gemma 3, Gemini 3 Pro, and gpt-oss-20b.

llm_is_primary_system.note: Generative LLMs are evaluated as primary benchmark systems alongside auxiliary fine-tuned BERT baselines.

arts_humanities_data.note: Utilizes Czech historical documents and periodicals, mapping to Language and Linguistics, and History.

quantitative_metrics.note: Employs Precision, Recall, F1 score, Intersection over Union (IoU), and Krippendorff's alpha for inter-annotator agreement.

published_after_march_2023.note: Published as a preprint in March 2026.

english_language.note: The paper is written in English, although the underlying benchmark text data is in Czech.

summary: This paper presents CzechTopic, a novel human-annotated benchmark for zero-shot topic localization in historical Czech documents. Grounded in inter-annotator agreement rather than a single gold reference, the dataset contains 1,820 manually annotated (text, topic) pairs. The authors establish baseline performances for both state-of-the-art generative LLMs and specialized BERT-based cross-encoders fine-tuned on distilled data.
key_findings: LLMs demonstrate substantial performance variability, with the strongest model (GPT-5-2) achieving a word-level F1 of 61.1 and IoU of 48.7, which still lags significantly behind the human baseline (68.7 F1, 57.2 IoU). Specialized BERT-based models fine-tuned on distilled data remain highly competitive (achieving up to 48.3 word-level F1 with RobeCzech), outperforming several smaller open LLMs.
